seo.page_title: 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G import to NZ
seo.meta_description: Import a 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G to NZ with 62 kWh battery and up to 385 km WLTP range. NZ-based team checks battery SOH and total landed cost before you commit.
seo.h1_tag: Import a 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G to NZ
seo.og_title: 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G | Import to NZ
seo.og_description: Looking at a 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G? We help you import from Japan with battery SOH checked, clear landed cost, and support through NZ entry and compliance.
seo.primary_keyword: 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G import NZ
secondary_keywords: import electric car to New Zealand

blocks.hero_h1: Import a 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G to NZ
blocks.hero_subtitle: 62 kWh Nissan Leaf E+ G with up to 385 km WLTP range, quick 0–100 km/h in about 7 seconds, and room for five — imported from Japan with battery SOH checked for NZ use.
proof_points: Transparent pricing — compliance is part of the process
blocks.why_kiwis_buy: Kiwis pick the 2021 Leaf E+ G for its 62 kWh battery, WLTP range up to 385 km and the ability to charge at home for far less than filling a tank. It handles stop–start city traffic smoothly, yet still has enough real-world range for regular trips between nearby towns.

savings_block.headline: Typical savings vs NZ dealer pricing
savings_block.body: Recent 2021 Leaf E+ G imports from Japan usually land between about $32,000 and $42,000 depending on mileage, grade and battery SOH. A similar car on a NZ yard often carries a margin on top of that. When we price a car, we include auction price, shipping, insurance, NZ entry work and our fee so you can compare against local stock properly.

cta_form.headline: Find your Leaf E+ G — we'll do the searching
cta_form.subtext: Fill in your details to get started. We'll search the Japanese auctions and come back with real options and pricing by email.

blocks.video_caption: Here’s a 2021 Leaf E+ G walkaround, showing the 17-inch alloys, hatchback boot access and the simple EV charging ports you’ll use day to day.
blocks.main_usp_deep: The 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G steps up with a 62 kWh lithium-ion battery (usable around 59 kWh) and a 160 kW motor, giving you up to 385 km WLTP range and brisk 0–100 km/h in about 7 seconds. For NZ commuting, most owners see plenty of range for a full week of city use between charges, or comfortable return trips between nearby centres. ProPILOT, AEB and lane keep assist add modern safety support, while Apple CarPlay/Android Auto and heated seats make daily driving easier. Imported examples from Japan usually have clear battery SOH data available, so we can focus on higher-health cars.

buyer_persona.headline: Who is this car right for?
buyer_persona.body: You’re a commuter or small family who wants to slash fuel bills and charge at home, but still needs enough range for weekend trips. You’re less worried about badge prestige and more about battery SOH, safety tech and running costs. For that mix of needs, the 2021 Leaf E+ G is a solid match.

trust_company.headline: Why import with ImportYourCar.co.nz?
trust_company.body: We’re an NZ-based team that’s been importing cars from Japan since 2009. Over that time we’ve helped bring in thousands of vehicles, including many Nissan Leafs and other EVs. We share full auction sheets, photos and cost breakdowns before you decide to bid. Our role is to help you understand the trade-offs so you can choose the right car for how you drive in New Zealand.

car_specs_benefits.benefit: Gives enough capacity for most NZ daily driving plus regular out-of-town trips without constant charging.
Comfortable for longer commutes or weekend drives, even allowing for real-world reductions from hills, weather and speed.
Strong, smooth acceleration for motorway on-ramps and passing, with simple front-drive traction in wet conditions.
Lets you top up quickly at many existing NZ fast chargers, handy for road trips or days with heavy driving.

car_specs_benefits.spec: 5 seats, hatchback body, Apple CarPlay/Android Auto, heated seats, 17-inch alloys
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Practical for family use with easy boot access, modern phone connectivity and winter comfort for early starts.

cta_mid_page.headline: See what a Leaf E+ G would cost you landed
cta_mid_page.subtext: Tell us how you drive and your budget, and we’ll price up real cars from the Japanese auctions.
cta_mid_page.button_text: Let us calculate your weekly savings.

models_comparison.intro: Here’s how the 2021 Nissan Leaf E+ G typically lines up when you’re comparing options to import.

rows.notes: The E+ G with the 62 kWh battery appears from around 2019 onward, with 2021 cars benefiting from newer battery age and updated safety tech. Later build dates can help with future resale and may show slightly better SOH on average, but price is usually higher.

As a pure EV there’s no engine choice, but there are two main battery sizes in the Leaf range: smaller 40 kWh packs and the 62 kWh pack in the E+ models. The 62 kWh version suits longer commutes and more open-road use, while the 40 kWh cars are more city-focused and usually cheaper.

The E+ G grade typically adds the larger 62 kWh battery, 160 kW motor, 17-inch alloys and more safety and comfort features compared with lower trims. Some cars may have extra options like ProPILOT, upgraded audio or different interior trims, which we can confirm from the auction sheet.

2021 Leafs generally in most cases meet NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. As an EV there’s no Clean Car fee, and many buyers may qualify for a rebate depending on current government settings at the time of registration.

The 62 kWh Leaf E+ G is common enough in Japan that there’s usually a steady flow of cars, but not as many as the 40 kWh models. That means you may wait a little longer for the right combination of mileage, SOH and price, but it also helps avoid having to compromise too much on condition.

steps.title: Tell us what you're after
steps.body: Fill in the form or message us. We confirm your brief and give you a cost breakdown before anything moves forward.

steps.title: We search the auctions
steps.body: We monitor USS, TAA, HAA and others. For EVs like the Leaf E+ G, we look for battery SOH information and charging history where available on each shortlisted car.

steps.title: You approve, we bid
steps.body: We send you the auction sheet and our recommendation. Once you're happy, you pay a deposit and we bid on your behalf.

steps.title: Shipping to NZ
steps.body: Typically 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule. For less common models, allow a little extra time once the auction clears. We keep you updated along the way.

steps.title: Compliance and delivery
steps.body: The car goes through NZ entry certification and compliance. Once it's through, we arrange delivery or you can collect.

risks_solutions.intro: Importing a Leaf E+ G is straightforward, but it helps to understand the main risks and how we handle them.

items.risk: You’re worried about buying a Leaf with a tired battery that won’t hold range for long.
items.solution: We focus on cars with clear SOH information and avoid obvious high-degradation examples where that’s visible in the documentation. We share the same data we see so you can decide if the trade-off between price, mileage and SOH works for you.

items.risk: Hidden costs between the auction price and getting the car on your driveway.
items.solution: Before bidding, we outline the expected landed cost: auction price, agent fee, shipping, insurance, port charges, NZ entry work and on-road costs. That way you can compare against local cars and decide if importing still stacks up.

items.risk: Uncertainty about auction grades and what they really mean for condition.
items.solution: We explain the auction grade, translate key comments and highlight any notes about repairs, paintwork or interior wear. You get the auction sheet and photos so you’re not relying on a single number.

blocks.how_to_use_iyc: Tell us your budget, how far you drive most days and what range you’re comfortable with. We then shortlist suitable 2021 Leaf E+ Gs from the Japanese auctions, explain the pros and cons of each, and only bid once you’re comfortable with the numbers. If you decide it’s not the right time, that’s fine — there’s no pressure to proceed.

faq_block.q: What real-world range can I expect from a 2021 Leaf E+ G in NZ?
faq_block.a: The official WLTP figure is up to 385 km, but real-world range in NZ is usually lower. Many owners see around 260–320 km depending on SOH, driving style, speed, terrain and use of heating or air-con.

faq_block.q: How much does it cost to import a 2021 Leaf E+ G from Japan?
faq_block.a: Most 2021 Leaf E+ Gs we see land between about $32,000 and $42,000. The total depends on auction price, mileage, battery SOH, shipping, insurance, port charges, NZ entry work and our fee.

faq_block.q: Will a 2021 Leaf E+ G usually meet NZ compliance requirements?
faq_block.a: Based on auction documentation, this model usually meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. Final approval always depends on physical inspection at NZ entry certification.

faq_block.q: How long does it take from winning an auction to driving the car in NZ?
faq_block.a: From a successful bid, shipping is typically 2–4 weeks depending on sailings, plus time at the NZ port and entry certification. In practice, many buyers see around 6–10 weeks in total, but it can vary with shipping schedules and workshop capacity.

faq_block.q: How do auction grades relate to the condition of a Leaf E+ G?
faq_block.a: Auction grades give a quick view of exterior, interior and overall condition, but the comments and photos matter just as much. We translate key notes, flag any repairs or heavy wear, and send you the full sheet so you can judge whether the condition suits your expectations.

footer_cta.button_text: Let us calculate your weekly savings.

subscription_block.headline: Stay in the loop
subscription_block.subtext: Get updates on Leaf E+ listings, EV import tips and current landed price ranges direct to your inbox.

blocks.company_positioning: ImportYourCar.co.nz is an NZ-based import agent working directly with Japanese auctions since 2009. We focus on matching buyers with the right cars for NZ roads and rules, including EVs like the Nissan Leaf. Our role is to explain the options, share the documentation and manage the process from auction through to NZ entry.

landed_cost_breakdown.intro: Landed cost is the total to get your Leaf E+ G from a Japanese auction to ready for registration in NZ.

rows.notes: The hammer price you pay for the car in Japan, plus local auction and agent fees.
Transport from the Japanese port to NZ and marine insurance to cover the vehicle in transit.
Port charges, customs, cleaning if required, entry certification, basic checks and our service fee to coordinate the process.

auction_checklist.check: Battery SOH reading and any notes on charging history
auction_checklist.why: Gives a snapshot of remaining capacity and helps you judge how the car’s range will hold up over time.

auction_checklist.check: Auction grade and comments on exterior and interior
auction_checklist.why: Highlights any repairs, dents, scratches or heavy wear that might matter to you once the car is in NZ.

auction_checklist.check: Odometer reading and service records if listed
auction_checklist.why: Lower mileage and documented servicing can support better long-term reliability and resale value.

auction_checklist.check: Photos of underbody and wheel areas
auction_checklist.why: Helps spot signs of hard use, kerb damage or corrosion-prone areas before you commit to bidding.

auction_checklist.check: Options list: ProPILOT, safety systems, infotainment
auction_checklist.why: Confirms you’re getting the safety and comfort features you expect, such as AEB, lane keep assist and CarPlay/Android Auto.

not_for_you_block.headline: Who this may not suit
items: You regularly drive very long distances in one hit and don’t want to plan around fast charging stops.
You need to tow heavy loads, which the Leaf isn’t designed for.
You prefer a plug type other than CHAdeMO and don’t want to rely on adapters or specific charging networks.
You want a brand-new EV with full NZ new warranty support rather than a used import.

alternatives_block.intro: If the 62 kWh Leaf E+ G doesn’t quite line up with your needs, there are other EVs worth considering.

rows.model: Nissan Leaf 40 kWh (X or G grades)
rows.better_if: You mainly drive in the city, have shorter commutes and want a lower purchase price.
rows.tradeoff: Less range than the 62 kWh E+ models, so open-road flexibility is reduced.

rows.better_if: You want strong efficiency and a more sedan-like shape for motorway work.
rows.tradeoff: Different charging port type and boot layout; availability and pricing can vary more at auction.

rows.better_if: You want a newer platform with longer range and access to the Tesla Supercharger network.
rows.tradeoff: Higher purchase price and different service and parts considerations compared with a Leaf.

battery_health_block.headline: Battery health: what to look for on a 2021 Leaf E+ G
battery_health_block.body: Battery state of health (SOH) is central to how a 62 kWh Leaf E+ G will perform over the years. While we can’t test the car in person until it reaches NZ, Japanese auction sheets and service records often include SOH readings or notes that help us avoid obvious problem cars. We focus on finding examples with healthier batteries, then share the same information with you so you can decide if the trade-off between price and SOH makes sense.
checklist: Target higher SOH readings where listed, especially for cars with higher mileage.
Check for comments about frequent fast charging or heavy use that might speed up degradation.
Look at build date and first registration date to understand battery age, not just odometer.
Consider how much range you actually need day to day — a slightly lower SOH may still be fine for short commutes.
Plan for gradual capacity loss over time, even on a healthy 2021 pack.

charging_block.headline: Charging a Leaf E+ G in New Zealand
charging_block.home_charging: Most Leaf E+ G owners in NZ charge at home overnight using a dedicated wall unit or upgraded socket. With a 62 kWh battery, many drivers only need to plug in a few times a week, depending on commute length and SOH. Night-rate power can make each full charge cost far less than a tank of fuel in a similar-sized petrol car.
charging_block.public_charging: On the road, the Leaf E+ G uses CHAdeMO fast charging, which is already common across much of NZ’s public network. You can top up quickly on longer trips or when you’ve had a heavy driving day. Slower AC chargers are also widely available at workplaces, shopping areas and public car parks.
caveats: CHAdeMO is well supported now, but CCS is more common on newer EVs, so think about how long you plan to keep the car.
Fast charging too often can contribute to faster battery wear, so it’s best kept for trips rather than daily use.
Home charging may require an electrician to check your wiring and install suitable hardware, which adds some upfront cost.
